Esports Athletes Need Mental Health Monitoring

This title was summarized by AI from the post below.
Dr Dylan Poulus
1mo
  • Report this post

🚨 New esports mental health editorial 🚨 "Time to Monitor the Mental Health of Esports Athletes" is now published in the British Journal of Sports Medicine (BJSM). Over the past few years, esports has attracted a level of institutional recognition that would have seemed unlikely a decade ago, from the International Olympic Committee – IOC's Olympic Esports Series and Esports Week to the Esports World Cup and Esports Nations Cup (Esports Foundation), which have drawn millions of viewers globally. The competitive infrastructure around esports players is growing fast. But institutional recognition without a corresponding duty of care is an incomplete step forward. Here, we argue that stakeholders in sports medicine, public health, and esports must work together to extend structured mental health monitoring to those competing on digital fields. 💡 The core argument: Esports athletes train and compete under conditions that closely mirror those of traditional sports, including high-performance pressure, public scrutiny, short contracts, and blurred boundaries between training and recovery. Anxiety symptoms have been reported in 38–82% of players, and depressive symptoms in 25–37% in some samples. Yet unlike athletes in traditional sports, most esports players have no formal mental health surveillance system watching over them. The sports medicine community has already built tools and frameworks, such as the IOC consensus statement (Margo Mountjoy, Scott Sloan, Vincent Gouttebarge) on mental health and the Sports Mental Health Assessment Tool (SMHAT), that could, with adaptation, be extended to esports. Early initiatives from Riot Games, BLAST, The GG Project, and The FUSE Cup - International School Esports show integration is feasible. Alongside these, organisations such as Movember have been investing directly in esports-specific research and programs. What's missing is coordinated leadership to make it standard practice. Mental health monitoring is not an optional enhancement. It is a foundational component of athlete care, and esports athletes deserve the same standard. Huge thanks to my co-author, Simon Rice. 📄 Full paper: https://lnkd.in/g5gzg_nh Movember / Southern Cross University / University of Melbourne / Orygen

    After a nearly a decade flip-flopping between VR sports spectacles and a dedicated Esports Olympics that fell before the starting line, was there ever a viable pathway for competitive video games into the Olympic Movement? In my latest op-ed for THE ESPORTS ADVOCATE, I recap the Olympic-esports saga, while diving into the issues that made integration a Hurclean task: https://lnkd.in/egXCxNMg - ⚔️ Violent in-game content: The Olympic charter and its pursuit of "a peaceful society" was always framed at odds with the most popular esports genres. While an exact line was never drawn, taking out phrases like "double kill" would amount to cultural erasure for gamers, and even undeniably safe titles like Rocket League and Street Fighter were deemed too unrealistic compared to sports sims. - 💵 Commercial interests: Modern games didn't emerge from a vacuum. There is an unbroken thread (spanning decades!) linking strategy and roleplaying games to the success of League of Legends or Dota 2, which could not be further from the shameless cash grab that many "virtual sports" ended up being. - ✒️ IP rights: The IOC was publicly clear that it would never pay licensing fees to use video games in its events. Even for the doomed Olympic Esports Games, a "different approach with regard to the financing" was constantly pledged, but never detailed - despite the Asian Games offering a successful blueprint. While I have never had strong feelings towards esports' inclusion in the Olympic sphere, first exploring this particular rabbit hole (way back when I was a journalist for The Esports Observer) is actually how I ended up in a career as a public policy advisor! I'm certainly interested to see if the IOC president is serious when she says this particular Olympic flame is “very much alive,” but what's for sure is the fundamental mismatches between gaming and sports politics won't be solved in the next few months.

    The End of the Pointless Olympic Esports Saga? https://esportsadvocate.net

  • David NEICHEL

    • Report this post

    I see a lot of posts recently commenting the IOC retreat from olympic esports... 📰 I didn't understand what is new. It seems to come from a japanese outlet (source in comments) mentioning on May 3rd. an internal IOC letter from January 2026. IOC paused several initiatives including esports to focus on its core business. Most comments are about why the IOC had to back off and why they failed... I don't want to speculate here. The olympic movement has its own purpose and will continue to be successful. The esports games were an attempt for an extension and it is not existential for them. 💡 I believe it tells much more about esports than it tells about the Olympics. And behind the shiny facade, it is not very positive for our ecosystem. The joke about olympic esports for years was to question who needs the other? On the paper, esports doesn't need the olympic validation. We have everything: - massive growing and engaged audiences across the globe. - powerful actors such as publishers, robust organizers and plateforms like ESL FACEIT Group - EFG, established big esports teams, accomplished athletes.. - massive events, and even multiple games championships like the #EWC or the #ENC - and more than everything, esports is a reality for all the youth almost everywhere in the world, practicing it at amateur level. But what we witnessed as soon as the IOC announced the Olympic Esports games shows a different reality: - Institutional organisations and governements suddenly taking esports seriously - high interest by the ecosystem which desperately looked for external validation - enthusiastic reaction by the community, which was flattered to see their passion recognized. We should not be too happy about the "victory'. By default, it is underlining our own weaknesses. We need to ask ourselves, why institutional and governments do not take esports seriously, unless a third party like the IOC jumps in. The answer is simple, we haven't done our homework to establish the collective solutions and structures capable to bring esports to its full potential. We need to collectively take over seriously the main issues such as integrity and fair play, minor protection, players' protection, amateur scene structuration, diversity, education... Most publishers, with a few exceptions, do not engage in such topics. But it is getting out of control. Esports has become a real thing for billions of people. And we all struggle to embrace the collective practice of esports. We need endemic, self-regulatory, independant and collective solutions, such as Esports Integrity Commission that is regrouping all organizers. The olympic framework would not have solved that problem for us. It is never a good idea to take shortcuts. But if we leave the space empty, we will never get the recognition esports and communities require, and stay exposed to aggressive regulations.

    Innovative Programming: The Key to Creating Memorable Esports Events In esports, competition may be the headline act, but innovative programming is what transforms an event into a memorable experience. Great esports events are no longer just about matches on stage. Audiences now expect immersive fan zones, creator integrations, live analytics, interactive broadcasts, community tournaments, networking opportunities, and entertainment that extends beyond gameplay. Innovative programming keeps attendees engaged before, during, and after competition. Why does this matter? 🔹 Audience Engagement – Diverse programming creates multiple touchpoints for fans, whether they are hardcore spectators or casual attendees. 🔹 Commercial Value – Sponsors gain more activation opportunities through creative experiences, not just logo placement. 🔹 Community Building – Side events, meet-and-greets, and grassroots competitions help strengthen loyalty and belonging. 🔹 Competitive Differentiation – In a crowded market, the most innovative events stand out and become destination experiences. Research supports this direction. A 2025 study on esports fan engagement found that digital innovativeness significantly enhances fan identification and engagement behaviours, highlighting the value of innovative experiences in building stronger fan connections. The future of esports events belongs to organisers who understand that audiences don’t just want to watch but they want to participate, connect, and remember. Innovation in programming isn’t an extra anymore. It’s a necessity. Check out the study here: https://lnkd.in/er4-rPbB  #Esports #EventManagement #FanEngagement #Innovation #GamingIndustry #LiveEvents

    Exploring the Impact of Digital Innovativeness on Esports Fan Identification and Engagement: An Integrated Framework of Innovation Diffusion Theory and the Psychological Continuum Model - Fong-Jia Wang, Siu Ming Choi, Kuo-Feng Tseng, I-Wei Chung, 2025 journals.sagepub.com

    #esports #videogames #coreProduct of Inception Games There is often some confusion about what exactly we are building, so let me clarify. We previously operated under two separate banners, but moving forward, we have two core products under one unified umbrella: 1. Everyday Gaming This is the casual gaming experience you are used to on your mobile or PC. Simply put, it features gameplay, ads, and animations built for a global audience. 2. The All-in-One Esports Ecosystem This is for the player who wants to start a career but has zero earnings. We provide a structured path from zero to professional. We are currently working on a few specialized modules and are rolling them out step-by-step according to our master plan. Even without running any marketing campaigns, the anticipation is massive. Players from Europe, Dubai, Nepal, Saudi Arabia, Myanmar, Pakistan, Cambodia, England, the Philippines, Vietnam, China, and Malaysia are eagerly waiting, and we are already having 1-on-1 conversations with them. While the local PC market is relatively small, we are actively strategizing on how to elevate the PC gaming landscape here. Mobile Market is good enough. We are aiming to launch our Mini Scrim League just before Eid-ul-Adha, followed by the rollout of our dedicated tools. Everything is almost ready. People ask about the cost of building this system. Financially, it took around ~ 50 Lakh BDT. But the real cost? The unimaginable pressure my team and I have endured. Over the last 2-3 years, we’ve put in 16-18 hour days doing heavy R&D, running surveys, and figuring out exactly what this market needs and how it needs it. Foreign partners frequently ask for our players. We send them, but they often struggle to survive past a few days because global competitors (especially from regions like China) are incredibly advanced. However, the ones who do compete at that level are earning well. To bridge this gap, we are setting up training programs to fully upskill at least 100-200 local players. Gaming is a fiercely competitive market - playing professionally is not an easy job. Globally, esports World Cups boast massive prize pools of around 8,000 Crore BDT. Our local teams are participating in these arenas. Even finishing 15th or 20th brings home 10-15 Lakh BDT, which is finally starting to catch the attention of local corporations. This market is so massive that there is room for everyone. There are billions of gamers out there. If the gaming and esports sector in this country is going to experience a revolution, it will happen through our hands. If we can't do it, you can assume nothing will happen in this space here. we are building for a minimum of 50 countries globally. The last 1,095 days were incredibly tough. i and my team went through a lot. From zero to One was really hard. We wanted to shutdown but there is always a comeback story. The next 12 months will be even tougher. Trust in the Almighty. #inceptiongames #epsorts

    My respoce as ESF Chairman to the IOC abandoning esports. For over a decade, the global industry has explored how competitive gaming might integrate into traditional sporting frameworks. Recent developments within the International Olympic Committee — including the decision under President Kirsty Coventry to pause the work of its Esports Commission — signal an important moment of reflection at the highest institutional level. This is not a setback for esports.It is a clarification. Several years ago, we published Esports Is Not a Sport, outlining a fundamental thesis: Competitive gaming does not align structurally with traditional sport. Not because it lacks legitimacy — but because it operates under an entirely different foundation: • Intellectual property is owned by developers and publishers, not federations• Competitive ecosystems are global by design, not nation-bound• Game formats evolve continuously, not within fixed rule sets• Participation is digital-first, not venue-dependent These are not limitations.They are defining strengths — but they require a different governance model. The challenge was never about “including esports.”It was about recognising that a new category had already emerged. The Elympic Sports Federation was established with this understanding from inception. Our position has remained consistent: • Governance must protect IP sovereignty, not override it• Frameworks must unify fragmented ecosystems, not force alignment through legacy structures• Competitive legitimacy must be built around participation, safety, and scalability — not constrained by historical definitions This moment represents a natural evolution in thinking. As global institutions reassess their approach, the need for a purpose-built governance layer becomes increasingly clear. The path forward is not adaptation of the old. It is the establishment of the new. The ESF stands ready to contribute to that future — through independent governance, global alignment frameworks, and a structure designed specifically for emerging competitive systems. — Ivan BrinklowFounder & ChairmanElectronic Sports EntertainmentFounding ChairmanElympic Sports Federation

    Esports has an identity problem, and until we fix that, the business side will keep struggling. For years, we’ve tried to force esports into a traditional sports model. Jerseys. “Athletes”. But here’s the disconnect: Esports fans aren’t sports fans. They don’t follow teams the same way. They don’t build lifelong loyalty. They follow games, creators, moments, and culture. When the game shifts… they shift. Yet the industry keeps building like loyalty is guaranteed, and then wonders why: • Fans don’t stick • Trophies don’t convert to revenue • Organizations struggle to stay profitable We’re building for the wrong customer. Esports isn’t just competition. It’s entertainment, community, and culture first, competition second. And here’s the part most people miss: You don’t fix this overnight with branding or better broadcasts. You fix it by building real revenue engines that match how gamers actually behave. This is where I’ve been pushing hard: Live selling. Not as a silver bullet, but as a practical bridge. Because while we figure out identity, live selling: • Drives immediate revenue • Creates real interaction between creators and fans • Turns attention into profit (not just impressions) • Keeps companies alive long enough to evolve It works because it leans into what esports already is: Direct connection, personality driven, and community first. Stop trying to make esports look like traditional sports. Start building around what it actually is. That’s how we get to sustainability.

    𝗖𝗛𝗘𝗦𝗦 𝗜𝗦 𝗘𝗩𝗢𝗟𝗩𝗜𝗡𝗚 𝗙𝗥𝗢𝗠 𝗔𝗡 𝗢𝗩𝗘𝗥-𝗧𝗛𝗘-𝗕𝗢𝗔𝗥𝗗 𝗚𝗔𝗠𝗘 𝗜𝗡𝗧𝗢 𝗔 𝗗𝗜𝗚𝗜𝗧𝗔𝗟-𝗙𝗜𝗥𝗦𝗧 𝗦𝗣𝗘𝗖𝗧𝗔𝗧𝗢𝗥 𝗙𝗢𝗥𝗠𝗔𝗧 The way chess is being consumed is changing, and it is changing - FAST. For decades, chess was built around classical formats: long games, over the board play and a relatively niche audience. Today, it sits more naturally inside a wider digital entertainment ecosystem, somewhere between traditional sport and esports. What is driving this shift is not the game itself, but how it is packaged, distributed and experienced. Online platforms such as Chess.com have made chess instantly accessible at a global scale. Live streaming on Twitch and YouTube has turned matches into real-time entertainment, where commentary, pace, storytelling and personality matter almost as much as the moves on the board. In many ways, chess is becoming a spectator format, designed not only to be played, but to be watched. The surge in interest did not happen by accident. The 2019 pandemic accelerated digital consumption across almost every category, and chess benefited early. The mainstream exposure from Netflix’s "The Queen’s Gambit" introduced the game to entirely new audiences. Combined with the growth of streaming culture and creator-led content, chess found a natural place in the digital landscape. We are also seeing the rise of faster, more dynamic formats. Events such as the Champions Chess Tour helped professionalise online competition, and newer formats such as Freestyle Chess and Esports Foundation World Cup tournaments are pushing the game further toward digital-first viewing: shorter, more intense, and easier to follow. Chess has increasingly moved into mainstream culture through creators, streamers and public figures outside the game itself. Celebrities such as Mo Salah, Luka Doncic and Carlos Alcaraz have publicly shown their interest in chess, and footballers as Eberechi Eze promoting the latest New Balance football shoes have appeared in high-profile chess-related campaigns. Even when these moments are brief, they matter: they help move chess into broader popular attention. At the same time, a new layer of digital-first players is emerging, focused on content, community and distribution. Together, they are reshaping how the game reaches audiences, and how value is created around it. But why does this matter? Because chess fits the current media landscape unusually well. It is global, easy to stream, intellectually competitive and increasingly format-driven. What comes next is not a replacement of classical chess, but an expansion around it. - More hybrid formats - More crossover with entertainment and (e)sports - More emphasis on personalities, storytelling and audience experience Chess is no longer just a game. It’s becoming a digital-first format that is shaping the future of competitive entertainment. #chess #sportsbusiness #digitalmedia #esports Andreas Thome Lejla Thome Thome Partners
